serving.natural_language_portion: One appetizer plate of Chilli Honey Garlic Potatoes weighs approximately 250 g, typical for a shared starter serving at an Indian or Indo-Chinese restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Chilli Honey Garlic Potatoes exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy deep-fried potato entries (FDCID 11408, 11413) combined with HPB Singapore data for sweet-sauced potato dishes and ICMR-NIN data for Indian-Chinese potato preparations. Input record values (energy 260 kcal, protein 2.6 g, fat 15.2 g, carbohydrate 28.0 g, sugar 10.0 g, sodium 380 mg, dietary fibre 2.0 g) are consistent with this composite and are retained with medium confidence. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg confirmed as this is a plant-based dish with no animal fat in the sauce; this is a confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for comparable fried potato preparations.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried potato / french fries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chilli Honey Garlic Potatoes. GI of 72 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for deep-fried potato / french fries (GI ~72, high category). The honey-chilli sauce adds free sugars which may further elevate postprandial glucose response. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (72 × 70.0 g) / 100 = 50.4. Available carbohydrate per serving = 28.0 g/100g × 250 g serving = 70.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 380 mg/100g from soy sauce and chilli sauce; sugar 10 g/100g with only 2 g/100g dietary fibre
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to sodium content of 380 mg per 100 g; a standard 250 g serving delivers approximately 950 mg sodium, representing over 40% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg, and the high free-sugar content (10 g/100g) further elevates cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72), 28 g/100g carbohydrate, 10 g/100g free sugar from honey and chilli sauce, low dietary fibre (2 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high GI (~72), high free-sugar content from honey glaze (10 g/100g), and low dietary fibre (2 g/100g), resulting in a glycemic load of approximately 50 per serving; portion should be strictly limited or replaced with a lower-GI preparation such as baked sweet potato.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 15.2 g/100g, saturated fat 2.4 g/100g, trans fat 0.2 g/100g from deep-frying, sodium 380 mg/100g, high caloric density 260 kcal/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to the combination of deep-frying-derived trans fat (0.2 g/100g), elevated sodium (380 mg/100g), high total fat (15.2 g/100g), and high caloric density (260 kcal/100g), all of which contribute to atherogenic risk and blood pressure elevation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); potato is a nightshade vegetable containing solanine; high free sugar (10 g/100g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish as deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs, potato belongs to the nightshade family (solanine may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals), and the high free-sugar content (10 g/100g) further promotes systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Honey Garlic Potatoes is a popular Indo-Chinese appetizer dish consisting of julienned or strip-cut potatoes that are coated in cornstarch and deep-fried until crispy, then tossed in a glossy sauce made from honey, chilli sauce, soy sauce, garlic, and sometimes spring onions and sesame seeds. The dish originates from the Indian-Chinese culinary tradition and is widely served as a starter at Indian restaurants in Singapore, particularly those offering North Indian and Indo-Chinese menus. The texture is crispy on the outside with a soft potato interior, and the flavour profile is simultaneously sweet from honey, spicy from chilli, and savoury-umami from soy sauce and garlic. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 260 kcal, 2.6 g protein, 15.2 g fat (of which 2.4 g is saturated and 0.2 g is trans fat from deep-frying), 28 g carbohydrate, and 10 g free sugar. Sodium is elevated at 380 mg/100g due to soy sauce and chilli sauce. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 72 (high), making this dish clinically significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The dish is vegetarian and dairy-free but contains soy and sesame allergens. It is not suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to nightshade content and AGE formation from deep-frying.

Input record values are internally consistent and plausible for this dish type. GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved GI source entry (deep-fried potato, University of Sydney GI Database). Confidence is therefore medium.

GI of 72 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for deep-fried potato / french fries; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in approved sources. 6. The input record listed two portions both labelled with 250 g and both marked is_default=true; the serving size of 250 g (1 appetizer plate) is retained as the canonical default. 7. Cultural tags in the input record included restaurant-specific strings ('Curry Village-Authentic North Indian Cuisine - Boat Quay') which have been normalised to cuisine-level descriptors. 8. The dish is classified as Indian-Chinese (Indo-Chinese) fusion, originating from the Hakka Chinese immigrant community in India and popularised in Singapore.
